title: SOC 2 System Availability and Incident Response Evidence Collection

compliance.assurance_rationale: SOC 2 A1 — Reviewed: SPL provides contributing evidence for this regulatory requirement. The search query directly addresses the monitoring objective specified by the clause.
compliance.controlObjective: Evidence that SOC 2 A1 is enforced — Splunk UC-22.8.2: SOC 2 System Availability and Incident Response Evidence Collection.
compliance.evidenceArtifact: Saved search 'UC-22.8.2' running on index itsi_summary and supporting catalogue-defined data sources, archived to the restricted audit_evidence index (default 7-year retention). Auto-drafted — SME review required.

dataSources: `index=itsi_summary` (service_name, alert_value, health_score, severity_value, is_service_in_maintenance, _time); `` `notable` `` macro (status, closed_time, rule_name, _time)
app: Splunk IT Service Intelligence (Splunkbase 1841), Splunk Enterprise Security (Splunkbase 263)
spl:
index=itsi_summary is_service_in_maintenance=0 is_entity_in_maintenance=0
| bin _time span=5m
| stats avg(health_score) as health_score, max(severity_value) as peak_severity by _time, service_name
| timechart span=1h avg(health_score) by service_name
```
```spl
`notable` status="Closed" isnotnull(closed_time)
| eval mttr_sec=closed_time-_time
| stats avg(mttr_sec) as avg_mttr, perc95(mttr_sec) as p95_mttr, count as closed_incidents by rule_name
| eval avg_mttr_hours=round(avg_mttr/3600, 2)
| table rule_name, closed_incidents, avg_mttr_hours, p95_mttr
description: Pairs ITSI service health KPI time series with ES notable closure MTTR for availability plus incident-response effectiveness in one evidence trail.
value: Gives the responsible team auditor-ready evidence for SOC 2 compliance by automating regulatory compliance evidence using indexes itsi_summary. Replaces manual evidence gathering with a continuous Splunk search that produces structured, time-stamped artefacts suitable for regulatory examination and internal audit review.
grandmaExplanation: Pairs ITSI service health key numbers time series with ES notable closure MTTR for availability plus incident-response effectiveness in one evidence trail — so you can show exactly what is being watched and when something went wrong.
implementation: (1) Model production services in ITSI with KPIs tied to SLIs; (2) keep `itsi_summary` retention aligned with audit window; (3) validate `closed_time` field on notables (`| fieldsummary closed_time`); (4) pair A1 uptime panels with incident MTTR for the same services via lookup; (5) document maintenance windows with `is_service_in_maintenance`. Indexes required: `index=itsi_summary`.
detailedImplementation:
Prerequisites
• Install and configure the required add-on or app: Splunk IT Service Intelligence (Splunkbase 1841), Splunk Enterprise Security (Splunkbase 263).
• Ensure the following data sources are available: `index=itsi_summary` (service_name, alert_value, health_score, severity_value, is_service_in_maintenance, _time); `` `notable` `` macro (status, closed_time, rule_name, _time).
• For app installation, inputs.conf, and Splunk directory layout, see the Implementation guide: docs/implementation-guide.md

Step 1 — Configure data collection
(1) Model production services in ITSI with KPIs tied to SLIs; (2) keep `itsi_summary` retention aligned with audit window; (3) validate `closed_time` field on notables (`| fieldsummary closed_time`); (4) pair A1 uptime panels with incident MTTR for the same services via lookup; (5) document maintenance windows with `is_service_in_maintenance`.

Step 2 — Create the search and alert
Run the following SPL in Search (then save as report or alert; adjust time range and threshold as needed):

```spl
`notable` status="Closed" isnotnull(closed_time)
| eval mttr_sec=closed_time-_time
| stats avg(mttr_sec) as avg_mttr, perc95(mttr_sec) as p95_mttr, count as closed_incidents by rule_name
| eval avg_mttr_hours=round(avg_mttr/3600, 2)
| table rule_name, closed_incidents, avg_mttr_hours, p95_mttr
```

Understanding this SPL

**SOC 2 System Availability and Incident Response Evidence Collection (A1)** — Pairs ITSI service health KPI time series with ES notable closure MTTR for availability plus incident-response effectiveness in one evidence trail.

Documented **Data sources**: `index=itsi_summary` (service_name, alert_value, health_score, severity_value, is_service_in_maintenance, _time); `` `notable` `` macro (status, closed_time, rule_name, _time). **App/TA** (typical add-on context): Splunk IT Service Intelligence (Splunkbase 1841), Splunk Enterprise Security (Splunkbase 263). The SPL below should target the same indexes and sourcetypes you configured for that feed—rename `index=` / `sourcetype=` if your deployment differs.

**Pipeline walkthrough**

• Invokes macro `notable` — in Search, use the UI or expand to inspect the underlying SPL.
• `eval` defines or adjusts **mttr_sec** — applying business logic to classify, threshold, or derive compliance-relevant indicators.
• `stats` aggregates events by **rule_name**, producing one row per unique combination for trend analysis and threshold evaluation.
• `eval` defines or adjusts **avg_mttr_hours** — applying business logic to classify, threshold, or derive compliance-relevant indicators.

Output fields: _time, avg_mttr, avg_mttr_hours, closed_incidents, health_score, mttr_sec, p95_mttr, peak_severity.

Step 3 — Validate
Confirm that events are present in the index and that the search returns expected results. Compare with ES Incident Review for the relevant correlation. Verify field extractions and index permissions.

Step 4 — Operationalize
Add the search to a dashboard or set up alert actions (email, webhook, PagerDuty, etc.) as required. Document the use case in your runbook and assign an owner. Consider visualizations: Line chart (health_score by service), Bar chart (avg_mttr_hours by rule), Single value (peak_severity), Table (closed incidents).
visualization: Line chart (health_score by service), Bar chart (avg_mttr_hours by rule), Single value (peak_severity), Table (closed incidents).
knownFalsePositives: Control test failures during audit windows, immature control rollouts, or known compensating-control gaps.
